name: GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail
name_original: Despoblado de Vea por el río Linares – running ida y vuelta desde San Pedro Manrique
description: Jog this 8.8-mile (14.1 km) moderate trail from San Pedro Manrique to the abandoned village of Vea, following the Linares River.
region_intro: Jogging around San Pedro Manrique offers diverse natural landscapes in the province of Soria, Castile and León. The region is characterized by the Linares River, which provides scenic routes, and the more challenging terrain around Peña Isasa. Runners can explore a mix of accessible paths and trails with significant elevation gains, catering to various fitness levels. The area also features historical elements, including 16th and 17th-century architecture and castle remains, which can be incorporated into running excursions. Best jogging routes around San Pedro Manrique The most popular jogging route is GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail, a 8.8 miles (14.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 45 minutes to complete, following the Linares River. Another top favourite among local runners is Peña Isasa loop from Turruncún, a difficult 7.6 miles (12.3 km) path that offers demanding sections and significant elevation changes. Local runners also love the Peña Isasa – Senda de los Cuñados loop from Canteras, a 7.8 miles (12.6 km) trail leading through varied terrain with substantial climbs, often completed in about 1 hour 50 minutes. Jogging around San Pedro Manrique is defined by riverside paths along the Linares River, challenging loops around Peña Isasa, and trails through historical village settings. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate riverside runs to difficult mountain ascents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 30 runners have used komoot to explore San Pedro Manrique's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in San Pedro Manrique?
A: There are over 5 dedicated running routes around San Pedro Manrique, catering to various fitness levels. These include moderate riverside paths and more challenging mountain trails.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails around San Pedro Manrique?
A: The region offers a diverse mix of terrains. You'll find accessible paths along the Linares River, often paved, ideal for leisurely jogs. For more challenging runs, areas like Peña Isasa feature demanding loops with significant elevation gains, requiring sure-footedness and sturdy shoes. The trails range from moderate to difficult.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running routes in the area?
A: While many routes offer a challenge, the trails along the Linares River provide more accessible options. For instance, the "Despoblado de Vea by the río Linares" is a round trip suitable for all skill levels with easily accessible paths. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064104577/gr-86-san-pedro-manrique-to-vea-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail&lt;/a&gt; is also a moderate option, following the river for 8.8 miles (14.1 km).

Q: Can I find circular running routes in San Pedro Manrique?
A: Yes, there are several circular running routes available. For a challenging experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064099577/sierra-de-alcarama-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Alcarama Circular Route&lt;/a&gt;, which is a difficult 15.9 km path. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Isasa – Senda de los Cuñados loop from Canteras&lt;/a&gt;, covering 12.6 km with substantial climbs.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while running in San Pedro Manrique?
A: Many routes offer scenic views of the Linares River. For impressive natural landmarks, you can explore trails around &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839751" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Isasa&lt;/a&gt;, a prominent summit in the area. The region's diverse landscapes provide a picturesque backdrop for your run.

Q: Are the running trails around San Pedro Manrique suitable for families?
A: Yes, some trails are suitable for families, particularly the more accessible paths along the Linares River. These routes often feature mostly paved surfaces, making them easier for a wider range of ages and fitness levels. Always check the route's difficulty and terrain before heading out with children.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the jogging trails in San Pedro Manrique?
A: While specific regulations for each trail may vary, generally, dogs are welcome on many outdoor trails in natural areas like San Pedro Manrique. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or other trail users, and to clean up after them.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in San Pedro Manrique?
A: San Pedro Manrique offers good running conditions throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures for outdoor activities. Summers can be warm, so early morning or late evening runs are advisable. Winters can be colder, but trails remain accessible, offering a different kind of scenic beauty.

Q: Are there options for parking near the trailheads?
A: Yes, many running routes in and around San Pedro Manrique have accessible starting points with parking options. For routes originating directly from the town, you can typically find parking within the village. For trails starting in smaller villages or more remote areas, designated parking spots are often available near the trailheads.

Q: What are the typical lengths and durations of running routes in San Pedro Manrique?
A: Running routes in San Pedro Manrique vary significantly. You can find moderate trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064104577/gr-86-san-pedro-manrique-to-vea-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is 14.1 km and takes about 1 hour 45 minutes. More challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40011621" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Isasa loop from Préjano&lt;/a&gt;, are around 9.8 km but involve significant elevation gain, taking approximately 1 hour 45 minutes to complete.

Q: Where can I find refreshments or accommodation after my run?
A: San Pedro Manrique itself offers local amenities where you can find refreshments and accommodation. The village has a historical charm with houses from the 16th and 17th centuries, providing a pleasant setting to relax after your run. Smaller surrounding villages might also have limited options.

Q: Are there any less crowded running options for a more peaceful experience?
A: To find less crowded running options, consider exploring trails that venture further from the main village or popular starting points. The diverse network of paths, especially those leading through the natural landscapes away from the immediate riverfront, can offer a more solitary and peaceful running experience.

Q: What makes the running experience in San Pedro Manrique unique?
A: The running experience in San Pedro Manrique is unique due to its blend of natural beauty and historical charm. You can jog along the scenic Linares River, tackle challenging mountain trails around Peña Isasa, and pass through a village rich in history with 16th and 17th-century architecture and castle remains. This variety caters to both nature lovers and those interested in cultural exploration.

Q: Can I find challenging trail running options near San Pedro Manrique?
A: Absolutely. For those seeking a challenge, the area around Peña Isasa provides several demanding loops. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Isasa loop from Turruncún&lt;/a&gt; are classified as difficult, featuring significant elevation changes and requiring good to very good fitness. These trails offer a true trail running experience.
tips: Beautiful view of the Linares River when we walk along it, a transit area to see the uninhabited areas of Vea, Peñazcurna and Villarijo from San Pedro Manrique.
One of the many beautiful images that we will find walking along the banks of the Linares River. Passage area to reach the unpopulated area of Vea and the traditional mills.

After a spectacular walk through the heart of the Sierra de Alcarama and the Linares river valley, we find ourselves in the area with the highest density of abandoned towns in the province of Soria. In this case, in front of the depopulated area of Vea. In the past, the road was used to access the mills and crops that were along the river.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail?
faqs.answer: Parking is generally available within the village of San Pedro Manrique, which serves as the starting point for this trail. Look for suitable spots near the village center or the main access roads.

faqs.question: Is the GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no explicit restrictions mentioned, it's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially given the varied terrain and potential for wildlife. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for jogging this trail, offering milder temperatures. Summer can be very hot due to the arid nature of the landscape, while winter might present challenging conditions with colder weather and potentially slippery surfaces on the "abrupt relief."

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail. It is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: How long does it take to jog the GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail?
faqs.answer: The round trip from San Pedro Manrique to the Abandoned Village of Vea is approximately 14.1 kilometers (8.8 miles) and typically takes around 1 hour and 45 minutes to complete at a jogging pace, depending on your fitness level and stops.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail features an "abrupt relief" as it descends into the Linares river valley. You'll encounter paths with "carefully crafted stone walls" and areas of "ancient cultivation terraces." As you approach Vea, the terrain becomes more "abrupt and arid." While some sections may feel relatively stable, it's a varied and rugged surface, not continuously smooth, requiring attention to foot placement.

faqs.question: What notable features or sights can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers "spectacular" views of the Sierra de Alcarama and follows the Linares River. Key highlights include the Riverside Trail Along the Linares River, old mills, and the dramatic destination: the "recondite and inaccessible" Abandoned Village of Vea, nestled in a green oasis.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as "moderate" and requires "good fitness" for jogging the round trip. The varied and sometimes rugged terrain, with its "abrupt relief" and stone structures, means it's not ideal for absolute beginners. A reasonable level of physical condition and experience with trail running is recommended.

faqs.question: Is the trail a loop or an out-and-back route?
faqs.answer: The GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail, as described, is an out-and-back route. You will jog from San Pedro Manrique down to the Abandoned Village of Vea and then return along the same path.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a jog on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the "abrupt and arid" sections and the moderate difficulty, it's essential to bring plenty of water, especially during warmer months. Appropriate trail running shoes with good grip are recommended for the varied terrain. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is also advisable, as well as snacks for energy.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or pubs along the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail itself leads to the Abandoned Village of Vea, which does not have any services. You will need to bring all your supplies. Any cafes or pubs would be found back in San Pedro Manrique.

waypoints.name: Riverside Trail Along the Linares River
